mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers general wave phenomena (A), including wave classification, diffraction, interference, electromagnetic radiation, sound, water waves, and gravitational waves.
Directly covers sound waves (E), including frequency, pitch, loudness, resonance, the Doppler effect, infrasound, noise, and acoustical properties.
Directly covers light waves (C) and focusing/imaging (D), including optics, wave-particle duality, speed of light, and the quantum theory of light.
Addresses the quantum theory of light (C.8), including wave-particle duality, the superposition principle (A.4), and Heisenberg's uncertainty principle (C.8.b).
Explains Maxwell's electromagnetic theory (C.6) and Faraday's foundational work on electromagnetic wave generation and propagation (B).
Illustrates geometrical optics, lenses, mirrors, and optical systems (D.1), as well as radio telescopes relevant to electromagnetic wave detection (B.2.a).
Discusses gamma rays (B.2.g), sources of gamma radiation from radioactive nuclei (B.3.e), and synchrotron radiation (B.3.d).
Demonstrates wave interference and diffraction through X-ray and electron diffraction in crystalline materials (A.6, B.2.f).
Establishes the constancy of the velocity of light (C.2) and introduces gravitational waves as a type of wave phenomenon predicted by general relativity (A.2).
Illustrates lens-based imaging systems (D.1), the electromagnetic spectrum in microscopy (B.2), and wave phenomena like diffraction limits in optical instruments.
Provides broad coverage of wave motion fundamentals, electromagnetic waves, quantum mechanics, and the physics underlying all wave phenomena in this section.
Uses seismic wave propagation as an example of mechanical waves (A.2.a), including wave behaviour at boundaries such as reflection, refraction, and mode conversion (A.5).
